title: Design Patterns in Java
summary: GoF patterns applied in modern Java: creational, structural, and behavioral patterns with real Spring/Java examples.

sections.content:
## Why Design Patterns?

Design patterns are **reusable solutions to common problems**. They provide shared vocabulary and proven approaches. In Spring/Java, many patterns are used implicitly — recognizing them helps you understand the framework deeply.

### Three Categories
- **Creational** — how objects are created (Factory, Builder, Singleton)
- **Structural** — how objects are composed (Adapter, Decorator, Proxy)
- **Behavioral** — how objects communicate (Strategy, Observer, Command)

sections.title: GoF Pattern Categories

sections.title: Strategy Pattern — Runtime Algorithm Swapping
Proxy Pattern — Spring @Transactional
sections.content:
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Caller
    participant P as Spring Proxy
    participant S as UserService
    participant DB as Database
    C->>P: saveUser(user)
    P->>P: Begin Transaction
    P->>S: saveUser(user) [real call]
    S->>DB: INSERT
    DB-->>S: OK
    S-->>P: return
    P->>P: Commit Transaction
    P-->>C: return
    Note over P: On exception: Rollback

sections.question: When Spring applies @Transactional, which design pattern does it use?
options: Decorator — wraps the bean with transactional behavior
Proxy — creates a proxy object that intercepts method calls and manages transactions
Strategy — selects a transaction strategy at runtime
Factory — creates transaction objects on demand
sections.explanation: Spring uses the Proxy pattern for @Transactional. It creates either a JDK Dynamic Proxy (for interfaces) or CGLIB proxy (for classes) that wraps your bean. When a method is called, the proxy intercepts it, begins a transaction, calls the real method, then commits or rolls back.
